The **Zhou dynasty** ([/dʒoʊ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*JOH*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 周; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōu*, pronunciation: [[ʈʂóʊ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Mandarin "Help:IPA/Mandarin")) was a [royal dynasty of China](/wiki/Dynasties_of_China "Dynasties of China") that existed for 789 years from c. 1046 BC until 256 BC, the longest span of any dynasty in [Chinese history](/wiki/Chinese_history "Chinese history"). During the [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou") period (c. 1046 – 771 BC), the royal house, surnamed [Ji](/wiki/Ji_(Zhou_dynasty_ancestral_surname) "Ji (Zhou dynasty ancestral surname)"), had military control over territories centered on the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") valley and [North China Plain](/wiki/North_China_Plain "North China Plain"). Even as Zhou suzerainty became increasingly ceremonial over the following [Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Eastern_Zhou "Eastern Zhou") period (771–256 BC), the political system created by the Zhou royal house survived in some form for several additional centuries. A date of 1046 BC for the Zhou's establishment is supported by the [Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project](/wiki/Xia%E2%80%93Shang%E2%80%93Zhou_Chronology_Project "Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project") and David Pankenier,[[4]](#cite_note-6) but [David Nivison](/wiki/David_Nivison "David Nivison") and [Edward L. Shaughnessy](/wiki/Edward_L._Shaughnessy "Edward L. Shaughnessy") date the establishment to 1045 BC.[[5]](#cite_note-7)[[6]](#cite_note-8)

The latter Eastern Zhou period is itself roughly subdivided into two parts. During the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period") (c. 771 – c. 481 BC), power became increasingly decentralized as the authority of the royal house diminished. The [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period") (c. 475 – 221 BC) that followed saw large-scale warfare and consolidation among what had formerly been Zhou client states, until the Zhou were formally extinguished by the [state of Qin](/wiki/State_of_Qin "State of Qin") in 256 BC. The Qin ultimately founded the imperial [Qin dynasty](/wiki/Qin_dynasty "Qin dynasty") in 221 BC after [conquering all of China](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification").

The Zhou period is often considered to be the zenith for the craft of [Chinese bronzeware](/wiki/Chinese_bronzeware "Chinese bronzeware").[[7]](#cite_note-9) The latter Zhou period is also famous for the advent of three major Chinese philosophies: [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") and [Legalism](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)"). The Zhou dynasty also spans the period when the predominant form of [written Chinese](/wiki/Written_Chinese "Written Chinese") became [seal script](/wiki/Seal_script "Seal script"), which evolved from the earlier [oracle bone](/wiki/Oracle_bone_script "Oracle bone script") and [bronze scripts](/wiki/Bronze_script "Bronze script"). By the dynasty's end, an immature form of [clerical script](/wiki/Clerical_script "Clerical script") had also emerged.

## History

### Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation")]

See also: [Predynastic Zhou](/wiki/Predynastic_Zhou "Predynastic Zhou")

#### Traditional myth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Traditional myth")]

According to [Chinese mythology](/wiki/Chinese_mythology#Shang_dynasty "Chinese mythology"), the Zhou lineage began when [Jiang Yuan](/wiki/Jiang_Yuan "Jiang Yuan"), a consort of the legendary [Emperor Ku](/wiki/Emperor_Ku "Emperor Ku"), [miraculously conceived](/wiki/Miraculous_births "Miraculous births") a child, [Qi](/wiki/Hou_Ji "Hou Ji") "the Abandoned One", after stepping into the divine footprint of [Shangdi](/wiki/Shangdi "Shangdi").[[8]](#cite_note-ode2ji-10)[[9]](#cite_note-eb-11) Qi was a [culture hero](/wiki/Culture_hero "Culture hero") credited with surviving abandonment by his mother three times, and with greatly improving agriculture,[[8]](#cite_note-ode2ji-10) to the point where he was granted lordship over [Tai](/wiki/Tai_(city) "Tai (city)"), the [surname](/wiki/Chinese_surname "Chinese surname") [Ji](/wiki/Ji_(Zhou_dynasty_ancestral_surname) "Ji (Zhou dynasty ancestral surname)"), and the title [Houji](/wiki/Houji "Houji") "Lord of [Millet](/wiki/Millet "Millet")", by the [Emperor Shun](/wiki/Emperor_Shun "Emperor Shun").[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Records_of_the_Grand_Historian''"Annals_of_Zhou",_[httpsctextorgshijizhou-ben-jin4578_§3]<div_class="paragraphbreak"_style="margin-top:0.5em"></div><span_title="Literary_Chinese-language_text"><span_lang="lzh">帝舜曰：「棄，黎民始饑，爾后稷播時百穀。」封棄於邰，號曰后稷，別姓姬氏。</span></span>[[Category:Articles_containing_Literary_Chinese-language_text]]<div_class="paragraphbreak"_style="margin-top:0.5em"></div>"Emperor_Shun_said,_'Qi,_the_black-haired_people_begin_to_be_famished._Do_you,_Lord_of_Millet,_sow_in_their_seasons_the_various_kinds_of_grain.'_He_enfeoffed_Qi_at_Tai;_[Qi's]_title_was_Lord_of_Millet;_and_his_distinctive_surname_was_Ji."-12) He even received sacrifice as a [harvest god](/wiki/Agriculture_(Chinese_mythology) "Agriculture (Chinese mythology)"). The term *Houji* was probably a hereditary title attached to a lineage.

[Buzhu](/wiki/Buzhu "Buzhu")—Qi's son, or rather that of the *Houji*—is said to have abandoned his position as Agrarian Master (農師; *Nóngshī*) in old age and either he or his son [Ju](/wiki/Ji_Ju "Ji Ju") abandoned their tradition, living in the manner of the [Xirong](/wiki/Xirong "Xirong") and [Rongdi](/wiki/Rongdi "Rongdi") (see [Hua–Yi distinction](/wiki/Hua%E2%80%93Yi_distinction "Hua–Yi distinction")).[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTE''Records_of_the_Grand_Historian''"Annals_of_Zhou",_[httpctextorgdictionaryplifenid4579remapgb_§3]-13) Ju's son [Liu](/wiki/Gong_Liu "Gong Liu"),[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWu1982235-14) however, led his people to prosperity by restoring agriculture and settling them at a place called [Bin](/wiki/Bin_(city) "Bin (city)"),[[c]](#cite_note-16) which [his descendants](/wiki/Family_tree_of_ancient_Chinese_emperors#Zhou "Family tree of ancient Chinese emperors") ruled for generations. [Tai](/wiki/King_Tai_of_Zhou "King Tai of Zhou") later led the clan from Bin to Zhou, an area in the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") valley (modern [Qishan County](/wiki/Qishan_County "Qishan County")).

The duke passed over his two elder sons [Taibo](/wiki/Taibo_of_Wu "Taibo of Wu") and [Zhongyong](/wiki/Zhongyong_of_Wu "Zhongyong of Wu") to favor the younger [Jili](/wiki/King_Ji_of_Zhou "King Ji of Zhou"), a warrior in his own right. As a vassal of the Shang kings [Wu Yi](/wiki/Wu_Yi_of_Shang "Wu Yi of Shang") and [Wen Ding](/wiki/Wen_Ding "Wen Ding"), Jili went to conquer several [Xirong](/wiki/Xirong "Xirong") tribes before being treacherously killed by Shang forces. Taibo and Zhongyong had supposedly already fled to the Yangtze delta, where they established the [state of Wu](/wiki/Wu_(state) "Wu (state)") among the tribes there. Jili's son [Wen](/wiki/King_Wen_of_Zhou "King Wen of Zhou") bribed his way out of imprisonment and moved the Zhou capital to [Feng](/wiki/Fenghao "Fenghao") (present-day [Xi'an](/wiki/Xi%27an "Xi'an")). Around 1046 BC, Wen's son [Wu](/wiki/King_Wu_of_Zhou "King Wu of Zhou") and his ally [Jiang Ziya](/wiki/Jiang_Ziya "Jiang Ziya") led an army of 45,000 men and 300 [chariots](/wiki/Chariot_(China) "Chariot (China)") across the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") and defeated [Di Xin](/wiki/Di_Xin "Di Xin") at the [Battle of Muye](/wiki/Battle_of_Muye "Battle of Muye"), marking the beginning of the Zhou dynasty.[[d]](#cite_note-17) The Zhou enfeoffed a member of the defeated Shang royal family as the Duke of [Song](/wiki/Song_(state) "Song (state)"), which was held by descendants of the Shang royal family until its end. This practice was referred to as [Er Wang San Ke](/wiki/Er_Wang_San_Ke "Er Wang San Ke") [[zh](https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%8C%E7%8E%8B%E4%B8%89%E6%81%AA "zh:二王三恪")].

#### Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Culture")]

According to Nicholas Bodman, the Zhou appear to have spoken a language largely similar in vocabulary and syntax to that of the Shang;[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBodman198041"Moreover,_Shang_dynasty_Chinese_at_least_in_its_syntax_and_lexicon_seems_not_to_differ_basically_from_that_of_the_Zhou_dynasty_whose_language_is_amply_attested_in_inscriptions_on_bronze_vessels_and_which_was_transmitted_in_the_early_classical_literature."-18) a recent study by David McCraw, using lexical statistics, reached the same conclusion.[[15]](#cite_note-19) The Zhou emulated Shang cultural practices, possibly to legitimize their own rule,[[16]](#cite_note-20) and became the successors to Shang culture.[[17]](#cite_note-li2006-21) At the same time, the Zhou may also have been connected to the [Xirong](/wiki/Xirong "Xirong"), a broadly defined cultural group to the west of the Shang, which the Shang regarded as tributaries.[[18]](#cite_note-22)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] For example, the philosopher [Mencius](/wiki/Mencius "Mencius") (372–289 BC) acknowledged that [King Wen of Zhou](/wiki/King_Wen_of_Zhou "King Wen of Zhou") had ancestry from among the Xirong, as King Wen's descendants, the Zhou kings, claimed descent from [Hou Ji](/wiki/Hou_Ji "Hou Ji"), a legendary [culture hero](/wiki/Culture_hero "Culture hero") possibly related to the Xirong through his mother [Jiang Yuan](/wiki/Jiang_Yuan "Jiang Yuan").[[19]](#cite_note-23)[[20]](#cite_note-24)[[21]](#cite_note-pulleyblank1983-25)[[22]](#cite_note-Pulleyblank2000-26) Additionally, the late 4th-century BC *[Zuo Zhuan](/wiki/Zuo_Zhuan "Zuo Zhuan")* comments that the baron of Li Rong (驪戎男), after being defeated by [Jin](/wiki/Jin_(Chinese_state) "Jin (Chinese state)"), married his daughter [Li Ji](/wiki/Li_Ji_(concubine) "Li Ji (concubine)") off.[[23]](#cite_note-27)[[24]](#cite_note-Lee-28)[[e]](#cite_note-30) According to historian [Li Feng](/wiki/Li_Feng_(sinologist) "Li Feng (sinologist)"), the term "Rong" during the Western Zhou period was likely used to designate political and military adversaries rather than cultural and ethnic "others".[[17]](#cite_note-li2006-21) Cultural artifacts of the Western Rong coexisted with Western Zhou bronzes, indicating close bonds between the Rong and the Western Zhou.[[26]](#cite_note-31)

### Western Zhou

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Western Zhou")]

Main article: [Western Zhou](/wiki/Western_Zhou "Western Zhou")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/EN-WesternZhouStates.jpg/500px-EN-WesternZhouStates.jpg)](/wiki/File:EN-WesternZhouStates.jpg)

States of the Western Zhou dynasty

During the Western Zhou (1045–771 BC), King Wu maintained the old capital for ceremonial purposes but constructed a new one for his palace and administration nearby at [Haojing](/wiki/Haojing "Haojing"). Although Wu's early death left a young and inexperienced heir, the [Duke of Zhou](/wiki/Duke_of_Zhou "Duke of Zhou") assisted his nephew [King Cheng](/wiki/King_Cheng_of_Zhou "King Cheng of Zhou") in consolidating royal power. Wary of the Duke of Zhou's increasing power, the "Three Guards", Zhou princes stationed on the eastern plain, [rose in rebellion](/wiki/Rebellion_of_the_Three_Guards "Rebellion of the Three Guards") against his regency. Even though they garnered the support of independent-minded nobles, Shang partisans, and several [Dongyi](/wiki/Dongyi "Dongyi") tribes, the Duke of Zhou quelled the rebellion, and further expanded the Zhou Kingdom into the east. To maintain Zhou authority over its greatly expanded territory and prevent other revolts, he set up the *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")* system.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChinn200743-32) Furthermore, he countered Zhou's crisis of legitimacy by expounding the doctrine of the [Mandate of Heaven](/wiki/Mandate_of_Heaven "Mandate of Heaven") while accommodating important Shang rituals at [Wangcheng](/wiki/Wangcheng_(Zhou_dynasty) "Wangcheng (Zhou dynasty)") and [Chengzhou](/wiki/Chengzhou "Chengzhou").[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaughnessy1999310–311Chinn200743Hucker197832–33-33)

Over time, this decentralized system became strained as the familial relationships between the Zhou kings and the regional dynasties thinned over the generations. Peripheral territories developed local power and prestige on par with that of the Zhou.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHucker197837-34)

The conflicts with nomadic tribes from the north and the northwest, variously known as the [Xianyun](/wiki/Xianyun "Xianyun"), [Guifang](/wiki/Guifang "Guifang"), or various "Rong" tribes, such as the [Xirong](/wiki/Xirong "Xirong"), [Shanrong](/wiki/Shanrong "Shanrong") or [Quanrong](/wiki/Quanrong "Quanrong"), intensified towards the end of the Western Zhou period.[[30]](#cite_note-WT-35) These tribes are recorded as harassing Zhou territory, but at the time the Zhou were expanding northwards, encroaching on their traditional lands—especially the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") valley. Archaeologically, the Zhou expanded to the north and the northwest at the expense of the [Siwa culture](/wiki/Siwa_culture "Siwa culture").[[30]](#cite_note-WT-35)

When [King You](/wiki/King_You_of_Zhou "King You of Zhou") demoted and exiled his [Qiang](/wiki/Qiang_(historical_people) "Qiang (historical people)") queen in favor of the commoner [Bao Si](/wiki/Bao_Si "Bao Si"), the disgraced queen's father the [Marquis of Shen](/wiki/Marquis_of_Shen "Marquis of Shen") joined with Zeng and the Quanrong. The Quanrong put an end to the Western Zhou in 771 BC, sacking the Zhou capital at [Haojing](/wiki/Haojing "Haojing") and killing the last Western Zhou king [You](/wiki/King_You_of_Zhou "King You of Zhou").[[30]](#cite_note-WT-35) With King You dead, a conclave of nobles met at [Shen](/wiki/State_of_Shen "State of Shen") and declared the Marquis's grandson [King Ping](/wiki/King_Ping_of_Zhou "King Ping of Zhou"). The capital was moved eastward to [Wangcheng](/wiki/Wangcheng_(Zhou_dynasty) "Wangcheng (Zhou dynasty)"), marking the beginning of the Eastern Zhou period.[[1]](#cite_note-Chengzhou-3)

The Zhou dynasty and contemporary polities c. 500 BC

The Eastern Zhou period (771–256 BC) was characterized by an accelerating collapse of royal authority, although the king's ritual importance enabled more than five additional centuries of rule. The *[Spring and Autumn Annals](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_Annals "Spring and Autumn Annals")*, the Confucian chronicle of the early years of this process, gave the period its name as the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period"). The [partition of Jin](/wiki/Partition_of_Jin "Partition of Jin") during the mid-5th century BC is a commonly cited as initiating the subsequent [Warring States period](/wiki/Warring_States_period "Warring States period").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHucker197837-34) In 403 BC, the Zhou court recognized [Han](/wiki/Han_(Warring_States) "Han (Warring States)"), [Zhao](/wiki/Zhao_(state) "Zhao (state)"), and [Wei](/wiki/Wei_(state) "Wei (state)") as fully independent states. In 344, [Duke Hui of Wei](/wiki/Duke_Hui_of_Wei "Duke Hui of Wei") was the first to claim the title of "king" for himself. Others followed, marking a turning point, as rulers did not even entertain the pretense of vassalage of the Zhou court, instead proclaiming themselves fully independent kingdoms. A series of states rose to prominence before each falling in turn, and in most of these conflicts Zhou was a minor player.

The last Zhou king is traditionally taken to be [Nan](/wiki/King_Nan_of_Zhou "King Nan of Zhou"), who was killed when [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") captured Wangcheng in 256 BC.[[1]](#cite_note-Chengzhou-3) [Duke Wen of Eastern Zhou](/wiki/Duke_Wen_of_Eastern_Zhou "Duke Wen of Eastern Zhou") declared himself to be "King Hui", but his splinter state was fully disassembled by 249. [Qin's wars of unification](/wiki/Qin%27s_wars_of_unification "Qin's wars of unification") concluded in 221 BC with [Qin Shi Huang](/wiki/Qin_Shi_Huang "Qin Shi Huang")'s annexation of [Qi](/wiki/Qi_(state) "Qi (state)").

The Eastern Zhou is also remembered as the golden age of Chinese philosophy: the [Hundred Schools of Thought](/wiki/Hundred_Schools_of_Thought "Hundred Schools of Thought") which flourished as rival lords patronized itinerant scholars is led by the example of Qi's [Jixia Academy](/wiki/Jixia_Academy "Jixia Academy"). The [Nine Schools of Thought](/wiki/Nine_Schools_of_Thought "Nine Schools of Thought") which came to dominate the others were [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism") as interpreted by [Mencius](/wiki/Mencius "Mencius") and others, [Legalism](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"), [Mohism](/wiki/Mohism "Mohism"), the utopian communalist [Agriculturalism](/wiki/Agriculturalism "Agriculturalism"), two strains of the [School of Diplomacy](/wiki/School_of_Diplomacy "School of Diplomacy"), the [School of Names](/wiki/School_of_Names "School of Names"), [Sun Tzu](/wiki/Sun_Tzu "Sun Tzu")'s [School of the Military](/wiki/School_of_the_Military_Strategists "School of the Military Strategists"), and the [School of Naturalists](/wiki/School_of_Naturalists "School of Naturalists").[[31]](#cite_note-chiphi-36) While only the first three of these would receive imperial patronage in later dynasties, doctrines from each influenced the others and Chinese society in sometimes unusual ways. The Mohists for instance found little interest in their praise of meritocracy but much acceptance for their mastery of defensive siege warfare; much later, however, their arguments against nepotism were used in favor of establishing the [imperial examination](/wiki/Imperial_examination "Imperial examination") system.

## Culture and society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Culture and society")]

The Zhou heartland was the [Wei River](/wiki/Wei_River "Wei River") valley; this remained their primary base of power after conquering the Shang.[[32]](#cite_note-37)

### Mandate of Heaven

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mandate of Heaven")]

Main article: [Mandate of Heaven](/wiki/Mandate_of_Heaven "Mandate of Heaven")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/%E9%99%95%E8%A5%BF%E6%89%B6%E9%A3%8E%E6%B3%95%E9%97%A8%E9%95%87%E5%BA%84%E7%99%BD%E6%9D%91-%E4%B8%89%E5%B9%B4%E5%85%B4%E5%A3%B6-%E8%A5%BF%E5%91%A8-%E5%AE%9D%E9%B8%A1%E5%91%A8%E5%8E%9F%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%99%A2.jpg/250px-%E9%99%95%E8%A5%BF%E6%89%B6%E9%A3%8E%E6%B3%95%E9%97%A8%E9%95%87%E5%BA%84%E7%99%BD%E6%9D%91-%E4%B8%89%E5%B9%B4%E5%85%B4%E5%A3%B6-%E8%A5%BF%E5%91%A8-%E5%AE%9D%E9%B8%A1%E5%91%A8%E5%8E%9F%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%99%A2.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E9%99%95%E8%A5%BF%E6%89%B6%E9%A3%8E%E6%B3%95%E9%97%A8%E9%95%87%E5%BA%84%E7%99%BD%E6%9D%91-%E4%B8%89%E5%B9%B4%E5%85%B4%E5%A3%B6-%E8%A5%BF%E5%91%A8-%E5%AE%9D%E9%B8%A1%E5%91%A8%E5%8E%9F%E5%8D%9A%E7%89%A9%E9%99%A2.jpg)

Western Zhou bronze pot (896 BC), Fufeng County, Shaanxi – Baoji Zhouyuan Museum

Zhou rulers introduced the Mandate of Heaven, which would prove to be among East Asia's most enduring political doctrines. According to the theory, Heaven imposed a mandate to replace the Shang with the Zhou, whose moral superiority justified seizing Shang wealth and territory in order to return good governance to the people.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETignor_et_al.2013-38)

The Mandate of Heaven was presented as a religious compact between the Zhou people and their supreme god in heaven. The Zhou agreed that since worldly affairs were supposed to align with those of the heavens, the heavens conferred legitimate power on only one person, the Zhou ruler. In return, the ruler was duty-bound to uphold heaven's principles of harmony and honor. Any ruler who failed in this duty, who let instability creep into earthly affairs, or who let his people suffer, would lose the mandate. Under this system, it was the prerogative of spiritual authority to withdraw support from any wayward ruler and to find another, more worthy one.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETignor_et_al.2013153-39) In this way, the Zhou sky god legitimized regime change.

In using this creed, the Zhou rulers had to acknowledge that any group of rulers, even they themselves, could be ousted if they lost the mandate of heaven because of improper practices. The book of odes written during the Zhou period clearly intoned this caution.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETignor_et_al.2013153-39)

The Zhou kings contended that heaven favored their triumph because the last Shang kings had been evil men whose policies brought pain to the people through waste and corruption.[[35]](#cite_note-40) After the Zhou came to power, the mandate became a political tool.

One of the duties and privileges of the king was to create a royal calendar. This official document defined times for undertaking agricultural activities and celebrating rituals. But unexpected events such as [solar eclipses](/wiki/Solar_eclipses "Solar eclipses") or natural calamities threw the ruling house's mandate into question. Since rulers claimed that their authority came from heaven, the Zhou made great efforts to gain accurate knowledge of the stars and to perfect the [astronomical](/wiki/Astronomical "Astronomical") system on which they based their calendar.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETignor_et_al.2013153-39)

Zhou legitimacy also arose indirectly from Shang material culture through the use of bronze ritual vessels, [statues](/wiki/Statues "Statues"), ornaments, and weapons. As the Zhou emulated the Shang's large scale production of ceremonial bronzes, they developed an extensive system of bronze metalworking that required a large force of tribute labor. Many of its members were Shang, who were sometimes forcibly transported to new Zhou to produce the bronze ritual objects which were then sold and distributed across the lands, symbolizing Zhou legitimacy.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETignor_et_al.2013153-39)

### Feudalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Feudalism")]

Further information: [List of Zhou dynasty states](/wiki/List_of_Zhou_dynasty_states "List of Zhou dynasty states")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/CMOC_Treasures_of_Ancient_China_exhibit_-_bronze_gui.jpg/250px-CMOC_Treasures_of_Ancient_China_exhibit_-_bronze_gui.jpg)](/wiki/File:CMOC_Treasures_of_Ancient_China_exhibit_-_bronze_gui.jpg)

A Western Zhou ceremonial bronze of cooking-vessel form inscribed to record that the King of Zhou gave a fiefdom to Shi You, ordering that he inherit the title as well as the land and people living there

Western writers often describe the Zhou period as feudal because the Zhou's *[fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian")* system invites comparison with European political systems during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Changshadragon.jpg/250px-Changshadragon.jpg)](/wiki/File:Changshadragon.jpg)

*[Silk painting depicting a man riding a dragon](/wiki/Silk_painting_depicting_a_man_riding_a_dragon "Silk painting depicting a man riding a dragon")*, painting on silk, dated to 5th–3rd century BC, from Zidanku Tomb no. 1 in [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha "Changsha"), Hunan

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Lacquer_painting_from_Ch%27u_State.jpg/250px-Lacquer_painting_from_Ch%27u_State.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lacquer_painting_from_Ch%27u_State.jpg)

A c. 316 BC [lacquerware](/wiki/Lacquerware "Lacquerware") painting from the Jingmen Tomb of the [state of Chu](/wiki/State_of_Chu "State of Chu") (704–223 BC), depicting men wearing precursors to *[hanfu](/wiki/Hanfu "Hanfu")* dress and riding in a two-horsed chariot

There were many similarities between the decentralized systems. When the dynasty was established, the conquered land was divided into hereditary fiefs (諸侯, *zhūhóu*) that eventually became powerful in their own right. In matters of inheritance, the Zhou dynasty recognized only patrilineal [primogeniture](/wiki/Primogeniture "Primogeniture") as legal.[[36]](#cite_note-41)[[37]](#cite_note-42) According to Hsi-Sheng Tao, "the Tsung-fa or descent line system has the following characteristics: patrilineal descent, patrilineal succession, patriarchate, sib-exogamy, and primogeniture"[[38]](#cite_note-43)

The system, also called "extensive stratified patrilineage", was defined by the anthropologist [Kwang-chih Chang](/wiki/Kwang-chih_Chang "Kwang-chih Chang") as "characterized by the fact that the eldest son of each generation formed the main of line descent and political authority, whereas the younger brothers were moved out to establish new lineages of lesser authority. The farther removed, the lesser the political authority". Ebrey defines the descent-line system as follows: "A great line (ta-tsung) is the line of eldest sons continuing indefinitely from a founding ancestor. A lesser line is the line of younger sons going back no more than five generations. Great lines and lesser lines continually spin off new lesser lines, founded by younger sons".

K.E. Brashier writes in his book "Ancestral Memory in Early China" about the tsung-fa system of patrilineal primogeniture: "The greater lineage, if it has survived, is the direct succession from father to eldest son and is not defined via the collateral shifts of the lesser lineages. In discussions that demarcate between trunk and collateral lines, the former is called a zong and the latter a zu, whereas the whole lineage is dubbed the shi. [...] On one hand, every son who is not the eldest and hence not heir to the lineage territory has the potential of becoming a progenitor and fostering a new trunk lineage (Ideally he would strike out to cultivate new lineage territory). [...] According to the Zou commentary, the [son of heaven](/wiki/Son_of_heaven "Son of heaven") divided land among his feudal lords, his feudal lords divided the land among their dependent families and so forth down the pecking order to the officers who had their dependent kin and the commoners who "each had his apportioned relations and all had their graded precedence""[[39]](#cite_note-44)

This type of unilineal descent-group later became the model of the Korean family through the influence of [Neo-Confucianism](/wiki/Neo-Confucianism "Neo-Confucianism"), as [Zhu Xi](/wiki/Zhu_Xi "Zhu Xi") and others advocated its re-establishment in China.[[40]](#cite_note-45)

#### *Fengjian* system and bureaucracy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Fengjian system and bureaucracy")]

There were [five peerage ranks](/wiki/Chinese_nobility#Peer_ranks_of_the_Zhou_dynasty "Chinese nobility") below the royal ranks, in descending order with common English translations: *gōng* 公 "duke", *hóu* 侯 "marquis", *bó* 伯 "count", *zǐ* 子 "viscount", and *nán* 男 "baron".[[41]](#cite_note-46) At times, a vigorous duke would take power from his nobles and centralize the state. Centralization became more necessary as the states began to war among themselves and decentralization encouraged more war. If a duke took power from his nobles, the state would have to be administered bureaucratically by appointed officials.

Despite these similarities, there are a number of important differences from medieval Europe. One obvious difference is that the Zhou ruled from walled cities rather than castles. Another was China's distinct class system, which lacked an organized clergy but saw Shang-descent yeomen become masters of ritual and ceremony, as well as astronomy, state affairs and ancient canons, known as *ru* (儒).[[42]](#cite_note-47) When a dukedom was centralized, these people would find employment as government officials or officers. These hereditary classes were similar to Western knights in status and breeding, but unlike the European equivalent, they were expected to be something of a scholar instead of a warrior. Being appointed, they could move from one state to another. Some would travel from state to state peddling schemes of administrative or military reform. Those who could not find employment would often end up teaching young men who aspired to official status. The most famous of these was [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius"), who taught a system of mutual duty between superiors and inferiors. In contrast, the [Legalists](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)") had no time for Confucian virtue and advocated a system of strict laws and harsh punishments.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Agriculture, Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Agriculture, Industry")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg/250px-Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shi_Qiang_pan.jpg)

The [Shi Qiang pan](/wiki/Shi_Qiang_pan "Shi Qiang pan") (c. 10th century BC), inscribed with the accomplishments of the earliest Zhou kings

Across the Zhou period, agricultural land was generally tied to aristocratic lineages rather than held as private noble property; for the Western Zhou in particular, this authority was rooted in lineage‑based political geography rather than in the later conceptualized [fengjian](/wiki/Fengjian "Fengjian") system.[[43]](#cite_note-LiFeng2006-48) Farming was carried out mainly by ordinary rural households, who formed the core of agricultural production.[[44]](#cite_note-LiFeng2013-49) These households owed labor service, taxes, and military obligations to their lords or to the state, depending on the period.[[45]](#cite_note-50) Various dependent laborers and slaves also existed, but they constituted a minority of the rural population.[[46]](#cite_note-PatriciaEbrey2010-51) Modern scholarship emphasizes that Zhou rural society did not resemble European manorial serfdom, since most farmers were not legally bound to noble estates and retained household autonomy.[[46]](#cite_note-PatriciaEbrey2010-51)[[44]](#cite_note-LiFeng2013-49) Classical texts describe an idealized [well field system](/wiki/Well_field_system "Well field system") (井田制) in which a plot of land was divided into nine squares, with the central square farmed for public obligations—such as producing grain for communal storage in times of poor harvest—while the surrounding squares were farmed by individual households.[[47]](#cite_note-52) Modern historians generally view this as a later conceptual model rather than a documented administrative system of the Zhou period.[[43]](#cite_note-LiFeng2006-48)

Some important manufacturing sectors during this period included bronze smelting, which was integral to making weapons and farming tools. These industries were dominated by the nobility who directed the production of such materials.[[48]](#cite_note-53)

China's first projects of [hydraulic engineering](/wiki/Hydraulic_engineering "Hydraulic engineering") were initiated during the Zhou dynasty, ultimately as a means to aid agricultural irrigation. [Sunshu Ao](/wiki/Sunshu_Ao "Sunshu Ao"), the Chancellor of [Wei](/wiki/Wei_(state) "Wei (state)") who served [King Zhuang of Chu](/wiki/King_Zhuang_of_Chu "King Zhuang of Chu"), dammed a river to create an enormous irrigation reservoir in modern-day northern [Anhui](/wiki/Anhui "Anhui") province. For this, Sunshu is credited as China's first hydraulic engineer. The later Wei statesman [Ximen Bao](/wiki/Ximen_Bao "Ximen Bao"), who served [Marquis Wen of Wei](/wiki/Marquis_Wen_of_Wei "Marquis Wen of Wei") (445–396 BC), was the first hydraulic engineer of China to have created a large irrigation canal system. As the main focus of his grandiose project, his canal work eventually diverted the waters of the entire [Zhang River](/wiki/Zhang_River "Zhang River") to a spot further up the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Military of the Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Military_of_the_Zhou_dynasty "Military of the Zhou dynasty")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Cavalry_and_Horse_Warring_States_period_475-221_BCE_Terracotta_Warriors_2013_exhibit_at_San_Francisco%27s_Asian_Art_Museum_20130320-110726_C4A.jpg/250px-Cavalry_and_Horse_Warring_States_period_475-221_BCE_Terracotta_Warriors_2013_exhibit_at_San_Francisco%27s_Asian_Art_Museum_20130320-110726_C4A.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cavalry_and_Horse_Warring_States_period_475-221_BCE_Terracotta_Warriors_2013_exhibit_at_San_Francisco%27s_Asian_Art_Museum_20130320-110726_C4A.jpg)

The [Taerpo Horserider](/wiki/Taerpo_Horserider "Taerpo Horserider"), a [Qin](/wiki/Qin_(state) "Qin (state)") terracotta figurine from a tomb near modern [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang") in [Shaanxi](/wiki/Shaanxi "Shaanxi"), 4th–3rd century BC[[49]](#cite_note-MK-54)

The early Western Zhou supported a strong army, split into two major units: "the Six Armies of the west" and "the Eight Armies of Chengzhou". The armies campaigned in the northern [Loess Plateau](/wiki/Loess_Plateau "Loess Plateau"), modern [Ningxia](/wiki/Ningxia "Ningxia") and the [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") floodplain. The military prowess of Zhou peaked during the 19th year of [King Zhao](/wiki/King_Zhao_of_Zhou "King Zhao of Zhou")'s reign, when the six armies were wiped out along with King Zhao on a campaign around the [Han River](/wiki/Han_River_(Hubei_and_Shaanxi) "Han River (Hubei and Shaanxi)"). Early Zhou kings were true commanders-in-chief.

King Zhao was famous for repeated campaigns in the [Yangtze](/wiki/Yangtze "Yangtze") region, and died on campaign. Later kings' campaigns were less effective. [King Li](/wiki/King_Li_of_Zhou "King Li of Zhou") led 14 armies against barbarians in the south, but failed to achieve any victory. [King Xuan](/wiki/King_Xuan_of_Zhou "King Xuan of Zhou") fought the [Quanrong](/wiki/Quanrong "Quanrong") nomads in vain. [King You](/wiki/King_You_of_Zhou "King You of Zhou") was killed by the Quanrong when Haojing was sacked. Although [chariots](/wiki/Chariot_(Ancient_China) "Chariot (Ancient China)") had been introduced to China during the Shang dynasty from Central Asia, the Zhou period saw the first major use of chariots in battle.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEbreyWalthallPalais200614-55)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaughnessy1988-56) Recent archaeological finds demonstrate similarities between [horse burials](/wiki/Horse_burial "Horse burial") of the Shang and Zhou dynasties with the steppe populations in the west, such as the [Saka](/wiki/Saka "Saka") and [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun").[[52]](#cite_note-KrechSteinicke-57)[[53]](#cite_note-58) Other possible cultural influences resulting from contact with these Iranic people of [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") in this period may include fighting styles, head-and-hooves burials, art motifs and myths.[[52]](#cite_note-KrechSteinicke-57)

The Zhou army also included "barbarian" troops such as the [Di people](/wiki/Beidi "Beidi"). King Hui of Zhou married a princess of the Red Di as a sign of appreciation for the importance of the Di troops.[[54]](#cite_note-59) King Xiang of Zhou also married a Di princess after receiving Di military support.[[55]](#cite_note-60)

The [Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng](/wiki/Bianzhong_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Bianzhong of Marquis Yi of Zeng"), a set of bronze *[bianzhong](/wiki/Bianzhong "Bianzhong")* percussion instruments from [his tomb](/wiki/Tomb_of_Marquis_Yi_of_Zeng "Tomb of Marquis Yi of Zeng") in [Hubei](/wiki/Hubei "Hubei"), dated 433 BC

During the Zhou dynasty, the origins of native [Chinese philosophy](/wiki/Chinese_philosophy "Chinese philosophy") developed, its initial stages of development beginning in the 6th century BC. The greatest Chinese philosophers, those who made the greatest impact on later generations of Chinese, were [Confucius](/wiki/Confucius "Confucius"), founder of [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), and [Laozi](/wiki/Laozi "Laozi"), founder of [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism"). Other philosophers of this era were [Mozi](/wiki/Mozi "Mozi"), founder of [Mohism](/wiki/Mohism "Mohism"); [Mencius](/wiki/Mencius "Mencius"), the "second sage" of Confucianism; [Shang Yang](/wiki/Shang_Yang "Shang Yang") and [Han Fei](/wiki/Han_Fei "Han Fei"), responsible for the development of ancient Chinese [Legalism](/wiki/Legalism_(Chinese_philosophy) "Legalism (Chinese philosophy)"); and [Xunzi](/wiki/Xunzi_(philosopher) "Xunzi (philosopher)"), who was arguably the center of ancient Chinese intellectual life during his time.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchirokauerBrown200625–47-62)

The state theology of the Zhou dynasty used concepts from the Shang dynasty and mostly referred to the Shang god, [Di](/wiki/Di_(Chinese_concept) "Di (Chinese concept)"), as [Tian](/wiki/Tian "Tian"), a more distant and unknowable concept, yet one that anyone could utilize, the opposite view of the Shang's spirituality.[[58]](#cite_note-63) The Zhou wanted to increase the number of enlightenment seekers, mystics, and those who would be interested in learning about such things as a way to further distance their people from the Shang-era paradigm and local traditions.

#### *Li*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Li")]

Having emerged during the Western Zhou, the [*li* ritual system](/wiki/Li_(Confucianism) "Li (Confucianism)") encoded an understanding of manners as an expression of the social hierarchy, ethics, and regulation concerning material life; the corresponding social practices became idealized within Confucian ideology.

The system was canonized in the *[Book of Rites](/wiki/Book_of_Rites "Book of Rites")*, *[Rites of Zhou](/wiki/Rites_of_Zhou "Rites of Zhou")*, and *[Etiquette and Ceremonial](/wiki/Etiquette_and_Ceremonial "Etiquette and Ceremonial")* compiled during the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") (202 BC – 220 AD), thus becoming the heart of the Chinese imperial ideology. While the system was initially a respected body of concrete regulations, the fragmentation of the Western Zhou period led the ritual to drift towards moralization and formalization in regard to:

* The five orders of [Chinese nobility](/wiki/Chinese_nobility "Chinese nobility")
* Ancestral temples (size, legitimate number of pavilions)
* Ceremonial regulations (number of [ritual vessels](/wiki/Ding_(vessel) "Ding (vessel)"), musical instruments, people in the dancing troupe)

### Sexuality

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Sexuality")]

Aside from *Shi Jing*, the earliest Chinese poem anthology, where gender-ambiguity and same-sex affection both made an appearance, the Zhou dynasty involved many recorded forms of homosexuality, including farmers and soldiers.[[59]](#cite_note-64) Bisexuality and/or homosexual practices often involved heterosexual marriage, foundational to kinship and social networks in the Zhou Dynasty and beyond in Imperial China, whereas male homosexuality was often "class-based," meaning these relationships involved economic and social benefits.[[60]](#cite_note-65)

## Kings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Kings")]

See also: [Family tree of Chinese monarchs (ancient) § Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Family_tree_of_Chinese_monarchs_(ancient)#Zhou_dynasty "Family tree of Chinese monarchs (ancient)")

The rulers of the Zhou dynasty were titled *wang* (王), which was also the term used by the Shang rulers, normally translated into English as 'king'.[[61]](#cite_note-66)
In addition to these rulers, King Wu's immediate ancestors—[Danfu](/wiki/Gugong_Danfu "Gugong Danfu"), [Jili](/wiki/King_Ji_of_Zhou "King Ji of Zhou"), and [Wen](/wiki/King_Wen_of_Zhou "King Wen of Zhou")—are also referred to as "Kings of Zhou", despite having been nominal vassals of the Shang kings.

| Wu 午 | [King Weilie](/wiki/King_Weilie_of_Zhou "King Weilie of Zhou") 周威烈王 | 425–402 BC |
| Jiao 驕 | [King An](/wiki/King_An_of_Zhou "King An of Zhou") 周安王 | 401–376 BC |
| Xi 喜 | [King Lie](/wiki/King_Lie_of_Zhou "King Lie of Zhou") 周烈王 | 375–369 BC |
| Bian 扁 | [King Xian](/wiki/King_Xian_of_Zhou "King Xian of Zhou") 周顯王 | 368–321 BC |
| Ding 定 | [King Shenjing](/wiki/King_Shenjing_of_Zhou "King Shenjing of Zhou") 周慎靚王 | 320–315 BC |
| Yan 延 | [King Nan](/wiki/King_Nan_of_Zhou "King Nan of Zhou") 周赧王 | 314–256 BC |

Nobles of the Ji family proclaimed Duke Hui of Eastern Zhou as King Nan's successor after their capital, Chengzhou, fell to Qin forces in 256 BC. Ji Zhao, a son of King Nan, led a resistance against Qin for five years. The dukedom fell in 249 BC. The remaining Ji family ruled [Yan](/wiki/Yan_(state) "Yan (state)") and [Wei](/wiki/Wei_(Spring_and_Autumn_period) "Wei (Spring and Autumn period)") until 209 BC.

During Confucius's lifetime in the Spring and Autumn period, Zhou kings had little power, and much administrative responsibility and [de-facto](/wiki/De-facto "De-facto") political strength was wielded by rulers of smaller domains and local community leaders.[[62]](#cite_note-70)

## Astrology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Astrology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Fittings_in_the_form_of_tigers%2C_Baoji%2C_Shaanxi_province%2C_Middle_Western_Zhou_dynasty%2C_c._900_BC%2C_bronze_-_Freer_Gallery_of_Art_-_DSC05756.JPG/250px-Fittings_in_the_form_of_tigers%2C_Baoji%2C_Shaanxi_province%2C_Middle_Western_Zhou_dynasty%2C_c._900_BC%2C_bronze_-_Freer_Gallery_of_Art_-_DSC05756.JPG)](/wiki/File:Fittings_in_the_form_of_tigers,_Baoji,_Shaanxi_province,_Middle_Western_Zhou_dynasty,_c._900_BC,_bronze_-_Freer_Gallery_of_Art_-_DSC05756.JPG)

Bronze fittings in the form of tigers, Baoji, Shaanxi – Western Zhou c. 900 BC

In traditional Chinese astrology, Zhou is represented by two stars, [Eta Capricorni](/wiki/Eta_Capricorni "Eta Capricorni") (週一; *Zhōu yī*; 'first star of Zhou') and [21 Capricorni](/wiki/21_Capricorni "21 Capricorni") (週二; *Zhōu'èr*; 'second star of Zhou'), in ["Twelve States"](/wiki/Girl_(Chinese_constellation) "Girl (Chinese constellation)") asterism.[[63]](#cite_note-71) Zhou is also represented by the star [Beta Serpentis](/wiki/Beta_Serpentis "Beta Serpentis") in asterism "Right Wall", [Heavenly Market enclosure](/wiki/Heavenly_Market_enclosure "Heavenly Market enclosure").[[64]](#cite_note-72)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Zhou_dynasty&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** [Fenghao](/wiki/Fenghao "Fenghao") is the modern name for the twin city formed by the Western Zhou capitals of [Haojing](/wiki/Haojing "Haojing") and Fengjing.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** The exact location of Wangcheng and its relation to Chengzhou is disputed. According to Xu Zhaofeng, "Chengzhou" and "Wangcheng" were originally synonymous and used to name the same capital city from 771 to 510 BC. "The creation of a distinction between Wangcheng and Chengzhou probably occurred during the reign of [King Jing](/wiki/King_Jing_of_Zhou_(Gai) "King Jing of Zhou (Gai)")", under whom a new capital "Chengzhou" was built to the east of the old city "Wangcheng". Nevertheless, the new Chengzhou was still sometimes called Wangcheng and vice versa, adding to the confusion.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** The exact location of Bin remains obscure, but it may have been close to [Linfen](/wiki/Linfen "Linfen") on the [Fen River](/wiki/Fen_River "Fen River") in present-day [Shanxi](/wiki/Shanxi "Shanxi").[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShaughnessy1999303Wu1982273-15)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-17)** Sima Qian was only able to establish historical dates after the time of the Gonghe Regency. Earlier dates, like that of 1046 BC for the Battle of Muye, are given in this article according to the Chinese government–sponsored [Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project](/wiki/Xia%E2%80%93Shang%E2%80%93Zhou_Chronology_Project "Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project"), but they remain contentious. Various historians have offered dates for the battle ranging between 1122 and 1027 BC.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-30)** [Eastern Wu](/wiki/Eastern_Wu "Eastern Wu") scholar-official [Wei Zhao](/wiki/Wei_Zhao_(Three_Kingdoms) "Wei Zhao (Three Kingdoms)") stated the Xianyu's rulers among the [Beidi](/wiki/Beidi "Beidi") were also surnamed Ji.[[25]](#cite_note-29)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-67)** Dates are those published by [Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project](/wiki/Xia%E2%80%93Shang%E2%80%93Zhou_Chronology_Project "Xia–Shang–Zhou Chronology Project") and Edward L. Shaughnessy's *The Absolute Chronology of the Western Zhou Dynasty*.
7. **[^](#cite_ref-68)** Or 周龔王
8. **[^](#cite_ref-69)** Or 周剌王
